title: Dental Caries -- Treatment / Management -- When do we restore?
content: “Noncleansable cavitated dentine caries cannot be managed by biofilm removal, remineralization, or sealing alone. However, in the primary dentition, these lesions might be transformable into cleansable lesions and managed via nonrestorative cavity control.” [2] “Certain occlusal lesions might appear clinically non-cavitated but radiographically extend significantly into dentine. If such lesions cannot be arrested through biofilm control alone, fissure sealing can be carried out; however, the integrity of the sealant needs to be monitored, and there is a possibility, until more evidence has emerged, that a “trampoline” effect may lead to failure of the sealant and restoration will be required.” [2]

title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
content: Dental sealants are recommended for both permanent and primary teeth. In permanent molars, resin sealants have been shown to reduce caries incidence for up to 4 years compared to no treatment. [10] The evidence on sealing cavitated caries is mixed; while some studies report success in arresting micro-cavitated lesions, others show limited efficacy beyond noncavitated stages. [11] [12]

title: Early Childhood Caries -- Treatment / Management -- Newer Treatment Approach
content: The traditional restorative and surgical way of managing early childhood caries requires general anesthesia with its attendant health risks and is expensive and does not prevent further caries but only treats the result of the disease. Therefore, up to 79% of patients treated with conventional therapy relapse. [32] This is why the new management of ECC focuses on preventive measures, including parental involvement, preventive programs with active monitoring of the condition, temporary restorations, and postpones advanced restorations.

title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Prognosis
content: Dental caries prognosis depends on the patient's health, maintenance of oral hygiene, and the extent and severity of the lesion. Ideally, full recovery of an initial caries lesion would be achieved with the described non-operative measures and are preferred. Despite this, a minimal intervention therapy can also be used, like pit and fissure sealants using resin-based or glass ionomer agents). Dentists should only consider restoring if the initial lesion progresses. [3]

title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
content: The use of sealants should be guided by individual caries risk, not just eruption status. Although no diagnostic tool perfectly predicts caries development, a history of caries remains the most reliable indicator of risk for caries development. Resin-based sealants are generally more effective than glass ionomer cements due to superior sealing ability. Nevertheless, glass ionomer materials still have a role, especially in situations where rubber dam isolation is not possible. Even when dentin is involved, sealing can be effective if a tight bond is achieved. In primary teeth, sealants may perform comparably well as more invasive procedures.

title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Clinical Significance
content: Extensive evidence supports sealants as an effective intervention for both preventing caries and arresting noncavitated lesions. One study in children demonstrated a 37% reduction in caries risk with pit and fissure sealants compared to a control group. Another study found that, over a 3-year period, first permanent molars treated with sealants showed a 44% lower risk of developing caries compared to untreated teeth. [3] Caries risk assessment remains essential in guiding clinical decisions, and each patient’s risk status should be reassessed periodically to optimize preventive care.

title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
content: Sealants can be applied to occlusal surfaces, as well as to smooth and proximal surfaces. Research supports their use in preventing both occlusal surface caries and arresting noncavitated and microcavitated lesions on proximal surfaces. [11] [13] [14] Sealants may be applied to both fully and partially erupted molars. However, retention is lower in the latter due to moisture control challenges, making glass ionomer a preferable choice in such cases. They are also effective on fluorotic teeth, with resin sealants showing significantly better retention than glass ionomer sealants after 1 year. [11]

title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
content: Slow-release fluoride beads are bonded to teeth to release low levels of fluoride in the oral cavity over a period of time, usually several years. Therefore, preventing dental caries and remineralizing incipient lesions. The 2 main types of slow-release fluoride devices are the copolymer membrane type and the glass bead type. Studies have shown that these devices raise intraoral fluoride concentrations enough to prevent enamel dissolution; however, further clinical trials are required to validate their use in clinical practice since the majority of research was done in vitro and in situ. [36] Although they may be a cost-effective solution for reducing dental caries in high-risk individuals, their retention rates are the main drawback. title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Introduction
content: Pit and fissure sealants act as a protective physical barrier, preventing the accumulation of food particles and microorganisms. Their use has proven effective in preventing the initiation of caries and arresting early noncavitated lesions. [3] [4] Among all teeth, permanent first molars exhibit the highest prevalence of caries, followed by second molars. These teeth are especially vulnerable because caries often begin shortly after eruption, and they are the first permanent molars to erupt. The long-term effectiveness of sealants relies heavily on their retention and correct application. [4]

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2670
content: Asymptomatic occlusion of basilar artery, a condition in which the basilar artery is blocked without producing noticeable symptoms, is evaluated in the context of a patient’s general wellbeing, with clinicians interpreting the significance of this silent vascular finding as part of an overall assessment of health status.

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2497
content: Cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم) is a condition in which the uterine cervix is unable to maintain closure during pregnancy, often leading to second‑trimester loss. Recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد) is identified as a parent or underlying factor linked to cervical insufficiency, underscoring the need to assess cervical competence in patients with habitual abortion. The cerclage (procedure) is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency as a therapeutic intervention that reinforces the cervix to prevent premature dilation and improve pregnancy outcomes. Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is also clinically associated with cervical insufficiency, indicating that cervical competence may influence the decision‑making and management of planned pregnancy termination. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose, counsel, and manage women at risk for cervical insufficiency and its related obstetric complications.
